coredata.dc:title: The role of center-N-doping in non-radiative recombination loss of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Xue, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: The C–N bonding environment reduces the integrity of the graphene lattice, induces defect states into nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), and significantly impacts the photoluminescence quantum yield of NGQDs. In the present work, we investigate non-radiative relaxation dynamics in pristine and N-doped GQDs based on Fermi's golden rule. Our results show that the N-doping perturbs the electron distribution of the frontier orbitals, which reduces the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) - lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) gaps and results in a redshift of the optical spectra of NGQDs compared to pristine GQD. In particular, the graphitic and pyridinic N create the midgap states but exhibit different effects on non-radiative decay in NGQDs. For the graphitic NGQD, the negative induction effect of graphitic N reduces the π-electron densities, giving rise to exceptionally small electronic couplings, especially during the electron trapping step; consequently, it has a less efficient non-radiative decay than the pyridinic and pyrrolic NGQDs. In contrast, the large intramolecular and solvent reorganization energies associated with the electron trapping step in pyridinic NGQD create a very efficient electron–hole recombination channel through the hole trapping step, accelerating its non-radiative decay. Overall, this work advances our current understanding of the specific role of each N functionality in the non-radiative decay of NGQDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of (a) pristine GQD, (b) pyridinic, (c) pyrrolic, and (d) graphitic NGQDs.

Frontier molecular orbitals (MOs), and HOMO and LUMO energies of (a) pristine GQD, (b) pyridinic, (c) pyrrolic, and (d) graphitic NGQDs. More frontier MOs are given in supplemental materials, Figure S1.

Projected density of states (PDOS) of (a) pyridinic, (b) pyrrolic, and (c) graphitic NGQDs, and (d) pristine GQD. The Fermi level is set to 0. The N-doping induces midgap states (TCB and TVB) into pyridinic and graphitic NGQDs, respectively.
Normalized absorption spectra of (a) pyridinic, (b) pyrrolic, and (c) graphitic NGQDs, and (d) pristine GQD.

Diagram illustrating the electron–hole recombination in pristine GQD and NGQDs. In the pyridinic NGQD, the electron trapping is expected to prevail over hole trapping due to the unoccupied midgap state. In graphitic NGQD, the hole trapping is expected to prevail over electron trapping due to the occupied midgap state.
Excitation energies, oscillation strengths and eigenvectors of main absorption peaks in pristine GQD and three NGQDs.

head.ce:title: The role of center-N-doping in non-radiative recombination loss of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, School of Textile Science and Engineering, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu Road, Binhu District, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Textile Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, School of Textile Science and Engineering, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu Road, Binhu District, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The C–N bonding environment reduces the integrity of the graphene lattice, induces defect states into nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), and significantly impacts the photoluminescence quantum yield of NGQDs. In the present work, we investigate non-radiative relaxation dynamics in pristine and N-doped GQDs based on Fermi's golden rule. Our results show that the N-doping perturbs the electron distribution of the frontier orbitals, which reduces the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) - lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) gaps and results in a redshift of the optical spectra of NGQDs compared to pristine GQD. In particular, the graphitic and pyridinic N create the midgap states but exhibit different effects on non-radiative decay in NGQDs. For the graphitic NGQD, the negative induction effect of graphitic N reduces the π-electron densities, giving rise to exceptionally small electronic couplings, especially during the electron trapping step; consequently, it has a less efficient non-radiative decay than the pyridinic and pyrrolic NGQDs. In contrast, the large intramolecular and solvent reorganization energies associated with the electron trapping step in pyridinic NGQD create a very efficient electron–hole recombination channel through the hole trapping step, accelerating its non-radiative decay. Overall, this work advances our current understanding of the specific role of each N functionality in the non-radiative decay of NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: The tunable electronic structures [1], low toxicity [2], chemical stability [3], and biocompatibility [4] of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have stimulated intense interest in the research community. Incorporating foreign atoms into a graphene lattice can be used to tune the semiconducting ability of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) by shifting the Fermi energy level upward or downward to open the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) – lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) gap. This tuning yields interesting photophysical properties that may be beneficial in applications such as photodetectors [5–8], photodiodes [9,10] and biotags [11,12]. The incorporation of foreign atoms induces different types of conductivities (p or n) into the graphene lattice. This process can be engineered to boost the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) efficiency. Furthermore, heteroatom doping creates structural defects such as nanoholes in graphene lattices and changes the delocalization of electrons and holes, which varies photoexcited charge carrier dynamics and can be adapted for various photovoltaic applications [13–22].

Boron and nitrogen atoms, sized similarly to carbon, are the ideal candidates for substitutional doping of graphene [23,24]. NGQDs can be synthesized by various strategies, such as stepwise solution chemistry [3], pyrolysis [25], electrochemistry [26], hydrothermal [27], and microwave methods [28]. Varying the experimental conditions, such as precursors, catalyst, and reaction temperature, produces NGQDs with different photophysical properties. Compared to the low quantum yield (QY) of pristine graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the N-doping improves the QYs of NGQDs by up to 94% [29]. However, despite such extraordinary performance, the mechanism of N-doping that might have contributed to the improvements in photoluminescence (PL) QYs of NGQDs remains ambiguous. Contradictory experimental observations exist regarding the effect of the C–N bonding configuration on luminescence QYs. For example, Permatasari et al. have found that the pyrrolic N is responsible for reduced PL intensity because it promotes non-radiative recombination [30]. However, Noor et al. have reported the opposite result: pyrrolic N promotes radiative recombination, increasing PL intensity [31]. Lv et al. have suggested that the graphitic N leads to reduced non-radiative recombination [32]. Santiago et al. have reported that increasing the concentration of pyridinic N promotes radiative recombination, thus improving the PL intensity [33]. These contradictory observations are due to the inhomogeneous size distribution of GQDs and the uncontrolled combination of different C–N configurations in the graphene lattice.

It is challenging to engineer a sample containing only a single C–N configuration in carbon materials experimentally; therefore, theoretical methods are the most viable way to address such issues. There are a few studies of the radiative electron–hole recombination in NGQDs using linear-response time-dependent density functional theory (LR-TDDFT) [3,34–38]. These studies attributed the enhanced PL intensities of NGQDs to the enhanced transition dipole moments of the high-lying excited states with significant oscillator strengths. However, this only takes into account the radiative transition of the PL QYs. If there are optically dark states below the high-lying excited states, the nonadiabatic transition proceeds the adiabatic transition of high-lying excited states, resulting in a non-radiative electron–hole recombination across the HOMO-LUMO gap and a low PL QY. For example, Niu et al. have reported that center N-doping increases the hybridization of the band–edge states and adds new energy levels inside the HOMO-LUMO gap of NGQDs, which promotes non-radiative electron–hole recombination and reduces the bright transitions [35]. Therefore, the nonadiabatic charge dynamics, i.e., non-radiative electron–hole recombination, of the low-lying excited state plays an essential role in the PL QYs of NGQDs.

Ab initio nonadiabatic molecular dynamics (NAMD) and Fermi's golden rule are the two most popular approaches to nonadiabatic transitions in nanomaterials. The former is based on the explicit solution of the time-dependent Schrödinger equation coupled with classical nuclear dynamics [39–41]. Among NAMD methods, Tully's fewest switched surface hopping (FSSH) is currently the most popular [39–45]. This method efficiently explores the nuclear phase space by selecting electronic potential surfaces using a stoichiometric procedure. However, for GQDs, exceptionally large exciton binding energy makes the practical implementation of this method very difficult. Although NAMD based on linear response theory, i.e., LR-NAMD, can address such an issue, the computational cost increases drastically with the number of nuclear trajectories due to the arduous procedure of solving the eigenvalue problem corresponding to the single-particle transition. The inclusion of decoherence in LR-NAMD further complicates the practical implementation of this method. In contrast, Fermi's golden rule, such as the Marcus equation and its derivatives [46–48], is computationally much cheaper than NAMD. The charge transfer occurs when the upper and lower diabatic states closely approach each other, where the charge transfer rates and electronic couplings can be explicitly obtained within the density functional theory (DFT) framework.

In the present work, we investigate how different C–N bonding configurations, that is, graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N, affect the non-radiative electron–hole recombination in NGQDs based on Fermi's golden rule. Previous work showed that center C–N configurations inserted new energy levels into the HOMO-LUMO gap of the GQDs, which blocks the radiative transition channels and induces non-radiative recombination centers into the GQDs [35]. However, the specific role played by each C–N configuration in non-radiative recombination remains unclear. Unraveling their differences helps to enhance the PL QYs of NGQDs with rationalized C–N configurations that can be produced experimentally [49].

The solvent reorganization energy and free energy change can be estimated by the state-specific approach, which assumes an equilibrium and a non-equilibrium solvent environments. In the equilibrium solvent environment, both the electronic and nuclear configurations of solvent are in equilibrium with the solute. In the non-equilibrium solvent environment, the electronic configuration of the solvent is in equilibrium with the final product state of solute, while the nuclear configuration of the solvent is in equilibrium with the initial reactant state of solute. Therefore, the free energy change is given by [52].

Rate equation (4) is suitable for simulating the system with the high-frequency vibrational mode in the low-temperature limit, i.e.,
k T ≪ ℏ ω
.

The geometries of GQDs and NGQDs were optimized using the Gaussian09 [54] software package at the DFT level of theory using the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE0) [55] functional with the 6–31g* basis set. A previous study has shown that combining this functional with this basis set provides a good description of excited energies compared to the experimental values [56]. In particular, the PBE0 functional can partially describe the charge transfer states [52]. After geometry optimizations, the state-specific LR-TDDFT calculations were carried out to obtain the free energy changes and solvent reorganization energies, based on equations (4) and (5), respectively. The intramolecular reorganization energies and effective Huang-Rhys factor were obtained by performing frequency calculations and then calculated using equations (7) - (10). Moreover, from the LR-TDDFT calculations, the dipole moments in the excited and ground states were extracted to calculate the electronic couplings based on equation (12). At last, the non-radiative rate constants were computed using rate expression (6). The non-radiative decay time is the inverse of the non-radiative rate constant. The contribution of the atomic species to the total density of states (DOS), i.e., PDOS, can be estimated as follows:

All calculations are performed with the conductor-like polarizable continuum model (CPCM) [59]. Dichloromethane is used as the solvent.

Fig. 3 shows the projected density of states (PDOS) for pristine GQD and NGQDs. The LUMO energy levels of pristine GQD and NGQDs are higher than that of TiO2 (∼-4.0 eV) and are in the order of graphitic NGQD (−2.69 eV) > pristine GQD (−2.73 eV) > pyrrolic NGQD (−3.46 eV) > pyridine NGQD (−3.84 eV). Such trend suggests the NGQD with graphitic N configuration provides more driving forces for the photoexcited electron transfer from the NGQD to the TiO2, facilitating the interfacial charge separation in NGQD-TiO2 composites. Such characteristic has been verified in Qu et al.‘s experiment that the graphitic N configuration greatly promotes electron transfer to TiO2, which decomposes rhodamine B much faster than before [65]. The calculated HOMO-LUMO gaps of pristine GQD and NGQDs are: pristine GQD (2.32 eV) > pyridinic NGQD (1.25 eV) > graphitic NGQD (1.15 eV) > pyrrolic NGQD (1.05 eV). This trend is consistent with a previous theoretical study, which suggested that center-N-doping reduces the HOMO-LUMO gap of the GQD [32]. In addition, the smallest HOMO-LUMO gap of pyrrolic NGQD coincides with the experimental observation that the emission spectra of NGQDs with dominant pyrrolic N-bonding configuration are red-shifted compared to the other N bonding configurations [31,62]. Such a small HOMO-LUMO gap increases the probability of non-radiative decay due to the decrease of activation energy (i.e., last exponential term in equation (4)), as observed experimentally [30,33]. In particular, the pyridinic N-doping creates an additional electronic level (
T
C B
) that is split from the conduction band (CB) edge of NGQD. In contrast, the graphitic N-doping creates an additional electronic level (
T
V B
) that is split from the valence band (VB) edge of NGQD, Fig. 2(a) and (b). Our result is similar to previous works that concluded that the midgap states could be introduced into the HOMO-LUMO gap of GQD via heteroatom doping [35,39,62,66]. These midgap states act as non-radiative recombination centers [67], which block the radiative transitions in the GQDs, rendering them non-fluorescent.

Fig. 4 shows the calculated optical spectra for pristine GQD and three NGQDs. The absorption spectra of the three different NGQDs are red-shifted compared to that of pristine GQD, consistent with experimental observations [68,69]. Increasing the concentration of nitrogen dopants causes more redshifts of absorption spectra, as discussed in Yang et al.‘s work.62 Nitrogen doping breaks the degeneracy of HOMO and LUMO orbitals, resulting in multiple low absorption peaks. The low excitations of the three NGQDs occur in the infrared region with weakened absorption strengths. The center-N-doping changes the local bonding environment of the carbon, which induces the hybridization of the frontier molecular orbitals (Fig. 2 (b)–(d) and Fig. 3 (a)–(c)). The hybridization of the frontier molecular orbitals alters the electronic structures of the NGQDs, resulting in a redshift of the spectra [35]. In the case of pristine GQD, the major absorption band (
S
0 → 3
and
S
0 → 4
) is composed of two highest occupied (HOMO-1 and HOMO) and two lowest unoccupied (LUMO and LUMO+1) molecular orbitals with π
→
π* character (Fig. 2(a)). In addition, there are two dark states below the lowest bright state, consistent with previous theoretical predictions [35,73] and experimental observation [74]. In the N-doped cases, the major absorption bands involve more orbitals. For example, the major absorption band of graphitic NGQD is composed of one prominent excitation S11 that is located around 2.12 eV, Table 1 . The S11 transition mainly consists of
H O M O − 1 → L U M O
and
H O M O → L U M O + 4
. The situation is similar for pyridinic and pyrrolic NGQDs. For pyridinic NGQD, the optical transitions corresponding to electron and hole trapping are
S
4 → 1
and
S
1 → 0
, respectively, while for graphitic NGQD, they are
S
6 → 1
and
S
1 → 0
, respectively. Even though the HOMO-LUMO gap of pyridinic NGQD is the largest one among the three NGQDs, its low-lying excitation energies resonate with those of the pyrrolic NGQD, indicating a more substantial exciton effect in this system.

The
S
1 → 0
transitions in the three NGQDs are optically dark. Eigenvector analysis of
S 1
states of three NGQDs shows that the nitrogen only contributes to the occupied energy levels of pyridinic NGQD. In comparison, the other two NGQDs have nitrogen contributions from both their occupied and unoccupied energy levels. To further investigate the optical origins of low-lying excited states associated with non-radiative recombination, the charge difference densities (CDDs) and charge transfer lengths of these states were analyzed. The electron density and hole density are equally distributed across the carbon skeleton of quantum dot (QD) without localization, and they are in good agreement with previous theoretical works [35,39,70]. This confirms that the PBE0 functional describes the excitation states of GQDs very well, and it can be trusted for the following calculations. The electrons and holes were not significantly separated in pristine GQD (Fig. 5 ) due to the π to π* transition. In the case of graphitic NGQD, the electrons migrate from the center to the edge upon photoexcitation, consistent with a previous theoretical report [62]. For pyrrolic and pyridinic NGQDs, a sizable hole density is localized around the vacancy defects while the electrons are mainly distributed at the exterior of dots, which enhances the electron-vibronic couplings in these two materials during non-radiative decay.

The charge transfer lengths of the
S 1
states are pyrrolic NGQD > pyridinic NGQD > graphitic NGQD > pristine GQD. Such a trend is caused by the extent of electron localization in these systems. A greater extent of electron localization in the graphene lattice causes a larger charge separation or vice versa upon the photoexcitation [70]. As shown in Fig. 2 and also illustrated above, the pyrrolic and pyridinic NGQDs have a greater localization of electron densities in their frontier molecular orbitals due to the N-doping, leading to larger charge transfer lengths. In addition, the electron and hole become more localized when transiting from the CB and VB edges of pyridinic and graphitic NGQDs to their midgap states, respectively, as indicated by their charge transfer lengths (
S
4 → 1
and
S
6 → 1
).

Fig. 6 outlines the electron–hole recombination pathways in the four systems. The pyridinic and graphitic NGQDs have midgap states inside their HOMO-LUMO gaps; therefore, their non-radiative recombination is divided into two subprocesses: electron trapping and hole trapping. The times of these two non-radiative decay processes determine the rate-limiting step for the preferential decay channel.

Table 2 lists the calculated results associated with the non-radiative recombination rate constant. The fluorescence spectra of the four molecular systems can be found in Figure S2. The band–edge excitonic states have shifted from S6 and S4 to S2 for graphitic NGQD and pyridinic NGQD, respectively, after the excited-state geometry optimizations. However, their orbital characters remain unchanged. The calculated rate constant, i.e.,
0.00222 ×
10 9
s
− 1
, for the lowest excited state
S 1
is much smaller than the experimental decay rate, i.e., 0.34–6.8 × 109 s-1, of the first absorption peak in pristine GQD [71,72]. However, be noted, the two lowest excited states are optically forbidden with zero oscillator strengths according to our calculation and other theoretical works [35,73], whereas the first absorption peak is optically active. These two optically dark states are caused by the structural symmetry, consistent with the previous report [75–77]. They leave no signatures for conventional optical spectroscopy, rendering them undetectable in the experiment. Upon further inspection, we found that the calculated optical gap energy of S3 is very close to the experimental optical gap energy of first absorption peak, i.e., 630 nm vs 660 nm, with large oscillator strength (3.21). In addition, the calculated rate constant of S3 state matched with the experimental decay rate of first absorption peak of pristine GQD very well. The strong oscillator strength and similar gap energy indicate that the S3 state corresponds to the first absorption peak in pristine GQD in the experiment. The two dark states below the S3 have long non-radiative decay times. These two states can become optically active through surface functionalization using oxygen-containing groups, such as epoxy, carboxylate and hydroxyl [70], which will be investigated in our future research.

The differences in total non-radiative decay rates between pristine GQD and pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic NGQDs can be explained by the free energy change, electronic coupling, intramolecular and solvent reorganization energies. The electronic coupling is proportional to the rate constant according to equation (4); therefore, a larger electronic coupling gives rise to faster non-radiative decay. The smaller free energy change, larger intramolecular and solvent reorganization energies reduce the vibrational quanta required for the effective vibrational mode [52]. Accordingly, it decreases the activation energy barrier, giving rise to faster non-radiative decay. The effective vibrational frequency is inversely proportional to the vibrational quanta [52]; therefore, a larger value of effective vibrational frequency decreases the activation energy barrier, facilitating the non-radiative transition. Furthermore, more high-frequency vibrational modes involved in a radiationless decay transition causes a faster electronic decoherence, which extends the exciton lifetime [78].

The calculated rate constants,
S
1 → 0
I I
and
S

,
of graphitic NGQD are much smaller than other NQDs due to their smaller electronic couplings. Such small electronic couplings are caused by the negative induction effect of graphitic N [79,80] rather than the reduced exciton effect, since the charge transfer length of
S 1
for graphitic NGQD is smaller than that of other NGQDs. However, the inclusion of graphitic N reduces the π-electron densities in the graphene lattice, reducing the electronic couplings between the frontier molecular orbitals. The total non-radiative decay time (
τ
S
2 → 1

+
τ

1 → 0
I I
) for electron and hole trapping is longer than that for direct recombination (
τ
S

I I
). However, the rate constant of
S
2 → 1
I I
is two orders of magnitude greater than that of
S
2 → 0
I I
due to a larger electronic coupling and smaller free energy change related to the
S 2
→
S 1
transition, suggesting a rapid internal conversion from the second-lowest excited state to the lowest excited state. In addition,
S
2 → 0
is coupled with more high-frequency phonon modes than
S
2 → 1
(see Figure S3), leading to the faster electronic decoherence effect in
S
2 → 0
. This indicates the electron–hole recombination in graphitic NGQD is mediated through a very efficient hole trapping process followed by a much slower electron trapping process, rather than direct band–edge recombination. The slow electron trapping process is the rate-limiting step of non-radiative relaxation in graphitic NGQD.

The exceptionally long non-radiative decay time (∼500 ns) suggests that the graphitic N suppresses non-radiative recombination in NGQDs, which is consistent with the experimental observation that the enhanced density of graphitic N enhances PL of NGQDs [81]. Be noted, the NGQD models employed in this study are used to illustrate the roles of different types of center-N-doping in non-radiative decay. In a synthesized NGQD, different N functionalities should coexist with one another; therefore, it is possible to have energy relaxations between different N functionalities both in the center and at the edges of GQDs, for example,
( graphtic N )
σ →
( pyridinic N )
π ∗
o r
( graphtic N )
σ →
( pyrrolic N )
π ∗
. The true non-radiative decay should be determined by the combined effect of the different N functionalities. Nevertheless, our results suggest that graphitic N can increase the PL QYs of NGQDs to a certain extent, which has also been observed experimentally [82,83].

The calculated rate constant for electron trapping (
τ
S
2 → 1
I I I
) is much greater than that of hole trapping (
τ
S
1 → 0
I I I
), indicating that hole trapping is the rate-limiting step for pyridinic NGQD, because of the larger electronic coupling and smaller free energy change of the electron trapping step compared to the hole trapping step. In addition,
S
1 → 0
I I I
is coupled with more high-frequency vibrational modes, leading to faster electronic decoherence and a longer exciton lifetime. The calculated rate constant of either electron or hole trapping is much greater than that of direct recombination in pyridinic NGQD (
S
2 → 0
I I I
), and the combined timescale of the two, that is,
τ
S
2 → 1
I I I
+
τ
S
1 → 0
I I I
, is much shorter than that of direct recombination,
τ
S
2 → 0
I I I
. This difference is caused by the smaller electronic coupling and larger free energy change associated with
S
2 → 0
I I I
, and suggests that the midgap states created by pyridinic N create a sufficient electron–hole recombination channel in GQDs. Therefore, similar to graphitic NGQD, the non-radiative decay in pyridinic NGQD proceeds through combined electron and hole trapping instead of direct band–edge recombination.

The overall non-radiative decay for pyridinic NGQDs proceeds more rapidly than pyrrolic NGQD, suggesting a less efficient non-radiative recombination in pyrrolic NGQD. This appears at first to contradict the experimental observations [30,33], which concluded that the PL intensities are proportional to the percentage of pyridinic N and inversely proportional to the percentage of pyrrolic N in NGQDs. However, the experimentally observed PL intensities result from the combined effects of center- and edge-N-doping in NGQDs. The center-N-doping in NGQDs exhibits a distinctly different effect on the frontier molecular orbitals (MOs) compared to edge-N-doping. The former induces midgap states, thus inserting non-radiative emission centers into NGQDs, while the latter barely affects the HOMO-LUMO gaps of NGQDs [35]. This difference may explain the discrepancies in the trends between our calculated results and the results from earlier experiments. Actually, Kabir et al. found that the low concentration of pyridinic N in NGQDs enhances the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) reactivity, which is indicative of high carrier density with low thermal deactivation that is associated with edge-N-doping [84]. The combined effect of center and edge-N-doping on non-radiative decay in NGQDs is a topic for further study.

For a pyrrolic NGQD, the electronic coupling of the S1 state is greater than that of a pyridinic NGQD, even though the smaller charge transfer length of the S1 state of a pyridinic NGQD suggests otherwise. This discrepancy is caused by the significant structural deformation of pyrrolic NGQD: larger structural distortion increases the electron–phonon coupling, resulting in a larger nonadiabatic vector in adiabatic space, that is,
φ i
|
∂
∂ t
|
φ f
[75]. When using the diabatic representation, the nonadiabatic vector is transformed into electronic coupling. As such, the larger electronic coupling of pyrrolic NGQD is caused by the phonon-assisted electronic transition. In addition, the electron-vibrational coupling spectrum for
S
1 → 0
of pyrrolic NGQD is broader than that of pyridinic NGQD (Fig. S2). This broadening indicates that the non-radiative decay process in pyrrolic NGQD has faster electronic decoherence that extends the exciton lifetimes [39,40]. The larger free energy change and faster decoherence leads to a longer non-radiative decay in pyrrolic NGQD.

Comparing the total non-radiative decay times between the three NGQDs and pristine GQD,
τ
g r a p h i t i c
>
τ
p r i s t i n e
>
τ
p y r r o l i c
>
τ
p y r i d i n i c
. This suggests the center pyrrolic and pyridinic N raise the non-radiative recombination in GQDs, whereas the graphitic N suppresses the non-radiative recombination in GQDs.

The electronic couplings play an essential role in all the QDs except for the graphitic NGQD. The electronic couplings between ground and excited states are related to the charge transfer lengths of photoexcited electron–hole pairs. The smaller distance between the electron and hole results in a larger overlap of the wavefunctions, leading to a stronger electronic coupling. One of the important functionalities of N-doping is to change the π-electron distribution of frontier MOs depending on the C–N bonding configuration, which leads to changes in electronic couplings.

In the present work, we conducted a detailed analysis of the non-radiative recombination in pristine GQD and NGQDs bearing different N functionalities, that is, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N using Fermi's golden rule approach. The N-doping changes the hybridization of frontier molecular orbitals in NGQDs, reducing the HOMO-LUMO gap and causing a redshift in the optical spectra. It also increases the separation distances between electrons and holes compared to the pristine GQD.

The electronic coupling has a significant effect on the non-radiative decay rates for pristine GQD and NGQDs. In addition, the graphitic and pyridinic N-doping induce additional energy levels that are split from the VB and CB, respectively, serving as the charge trapping centers in the two NGQDs. However, the calculated non-radiative decay rates of graphitic NGQD indicate a lack of efficient non-radiative decay in the QD due to the negative induction effect. In contrast, the pyridinic NGQD shows a faster non-radiative decay due to the efficient electron trapping process. The structural distortion increases the electronic coupling of lowest excited state of pyrrolic NGQD. However, the larger free energy change and dephasing effect cause a slower non-radiative decay of pyrrolic NGQD than pyridinic NGQD. This study offers insight into the electronic structures and non-radiative decay dynamics of NGQDs, which is useful for the rational design of photovoltaic devices.
